frameworks.name: corticocortical feedback
frameworks.one_line: Top-down feedback from extrastriate areas modulates V1 responses

frameworks.name: surround suppression
frameworks.one_line: Receptive-field surround reduces center response; feedback contributes to it

frameworks.name: reversible cortical inactivation
frameworks.one_line: Cooling V2/V3 to causally test feedback contribution to V1

core_question: Does corticocortical feedback from V2 and V3 contribute to surround suppression and other receptive-field properties in V1 of alert primates?
key_assumptions: Cortical cooling reversibly inactivates feedback without confounding effects
V1 receptive-field properties reflect contributions from both feedforward and feedback inputs
Alert-monkey recordings reveal feedback effects masked under anesthesia
manipulations: Reversible inactivation of V2 and V3 via cortical cooling
Stimulus diameter restricted to RF center vs extending beyond center
key_findings: Inactivating V2/V3 feedback produced both suppression and facilitation for center stimuli with small reductions in orientation selectivity
Removing feedback strongly facilitated responses to large stimuli, reducing surround suppression at both low and high contrast
Eliminating feedback reduced end-stopping for extended contours
Inactivation effects were largest in neurons with strongest baseline surround suppression and matched the timing of surround suppression
interpretations: Corticocortical feedback from V2 and V3 directly contributes to surround suppression in V1
Feedback influences are stronger and more diverse in alert than in anesthetized animals
